turns.author: Lauren Koff <lauren.koff@hcbe.net>
turns.body_text:
CAUTION: This email originated from outside of the organization. Do not click links or open attachments unless you recognize the sender and know the content is safe.
Hello,

I am looking for a manual on inventory. I am trying to figure out reasons inventory value would differ from market value.

I know market value is the value of the inventory on hand based on the current price. We have the method set to moving average price, however, one thing we have noticed is inventory value is sometimes higher than you would expect. (ie: Cereal was $50 a case, it is now $60 a case. If I brought in 1 case at the old price and one case at the new price, the averaged value would be likely $110 dollars. But the inventory value is higher than that?)

We are just looking to understand the differences.

Thank you.

Lauren Koff, MS, RD, LD, SNS
Dietitian
Houston County School Nutrition Program
1600 Macon Road
Suite C
Perry, Georgia 31069
(478) 322-3308

Hello Lauren,  Sorry it took a bit to get back to you but here are my findings for this particular case. The pickles item number 102535 shows that they were manually withdrawn due to the production withdraw for 10/2 not coming through which is shown below in the item history report. The issue lies on the single ingredient recipe that most likely used the pickles in question which was lr 1690 which if you look at the food required section of the production plan is empty due to the buying guide section not linking it to the stock items so it would not pull through onto the production withdraw since the system missing the item and weight to pull it though automatically and unless the item was manually added at that particular production withdraw (which it does not seem to be based on the withdrawal item list for 10/2 ) it would not pull the items from the perpetual until it was done manually. [image.png]

My question is, why is her perpetual inventory showing 8 cs?  I thought I was looking at the correct thing (Perpetual Inventory-Current) to see what she had on hand at that moment.

Hello Branden,     The Line items have different time/date stamps to explain what occurred with the items. Hello Shelby,     The site is not "actually" going into the negative... what you are seeing is based on time stamps in the system.   The yellow highlight shows the dates where you're seeing the effect of the time stamps... the red blocks show the withdrawal time stamps that are occurring after the receipt of the product is entered when it comes from the vendor.   The most evident will be on January 14th, as the withdrawals are backdated an entire day...  Looking at January 14th - you'll see that 3 cases were received from the vendor at 3:18pm. This was technically the "first" entry after the usage recorded on January 12, which left 1 case & 13 broken units in stock.   On January 15th, the additional withdrawals were entered with time stamps BEFORE the receipt, which causes the math to appear as though negative quantities occurred. The left column of dates/times is when the transactions "should have" taken place, but did not.   If you were to reorder the columns based on the time the user actually keyed in the transactions (2nd column - labeled "Transaction Date"), then there would be no appearance of negative numbers. There was a starting balance of 0 at the end of January 12 (blue outline in screenshot of report).     If users enter the receives at the time they arrive, they will not need to backdate their transactions. I'm sure the milk was received prior to the meal services in order to complete the services.                 Check out PrimeroEdge Training WEBINARS Available: Click Here  SchoolCafe Training WEBINARS Available: Click Here     Let us know if you have any questions regarding this ticket by

turns.body_html:
Hello Shelby,

The site is not "actually" going into the negative... what you are seeing is based on time stamps in the system. 

The yellow highlight shows the dates where you're seeing the effect of the time stamps... the red blocks show the withdrawal time stamps that are occurring after the receipt of the product is entered when it comes from the vendor. 

The most evident will be on January 14th, as the withdrawals are backdated an entire day...

Looking at January 14th - you'll see that 3 cases were received from the vendor at 3:18pm. This was technically the "first" entry after the usage recorded on January 12, which left 1 case & 13 broken units in stock. 

On January 15th, the additional withdrawals were entered with time stamps BEFORE the receipt, which causes the math to appear as though negative quantities occurred. The left column of dates/times is when the transactions "should have" taken place, but did not. 

If you were to reorder the columns based on the time the user actually keyed in the transactions (2nd column - labeled "Transaction Date"), then there would be no appearance of negative numbers. There was a starting balance of 0 at the end of January 12 (blue outline in screenshot of report).

If users enter the receives at the time they arrive, they will not need to backdate their transactions. I'm sure the milk was received prior to the meal services in order to complete the services.

Hello Nganhai,        The Adjustment column shows the change that was made. For example, an adjustment of 12 means that 12 broken units were removed. If the inventory was at zero before that day, removing 12 would result in a perpetual balance of -12.  So, the Adjustment column reflects what was changed, and the Perpetual column shows the inventory balance after that change.  The only exception is when the entry is labeled as a Reconciliation. Since physical inventory is not a manual override, the quantity entered becomes the new perpetual balance, and the adjustment reflects the difference between what was previously recorded and what was physically counted. turns.body_html:
I think the missing piece is that they pulled the report as of 3/1, but needed to pull it further back to see what actually occurred and where it all started from. 

This item was already a mess before 3/20. It was not starting clean at zero in any meaningful “safe to withdraw” way. There were already a ton of earlier backdated withdrawals and receipts affecting the running history for this item. The extended item history shows repeated negative perpetual balances and multiple receives/withdrawals well before the questioned transaction. On 3/2 at 12:52:31 PM, there was a Receive transaction for 5 WU, which brought the balance back to 0 WU / 0 BU. Then, later on 3/2, additional transactions were entered, including the questioned withdrawals that were actually entered on 3/20 around 2:24–2:26 PM. 

The system did not allow the withdrawal because it “ignored” a negative balance. It allowed it because the item history had additional inventory activity and the transaction was processed based on the balance available at the time it was entered, not just the customer’s isolated view of those two rows.

The customer is reading only these two lines below and thinking, "shouldn't that just equal -50?", but that's not how this report works. 

3/20 entry that removed 12 BU
3/20 entry that removed 38 BU

The Perpetual Balance is a running balance, not a standalone calculator per row. So each row reflects, what happened before it, whether there were receipts, whether there were other withdrawals, whether there were whole units vs broken , units, and whether inventory had already shifted between WU and BU. That’s why the customer’s “12 + 38 = -50” logic is too simplified for this report.

Hello Nganhai,

Thank you for your patience while we reviewed this further.

I’ve attached a screenshot from the item history report to help explain what occurred. It helps to pull a further date range to see what occurred. 


The main thing to note is that this report is showing two different date concepts:
  • Actual Transaction Date = the date the transaction is applied to in inventory history
  • Transaction Date = when the transaction was actually entered into the system
In the highlighted rows, you can see that the withdrawals in question were tied to 3/2, but they were actually entered on 3/20 at 2:24 PM and 2:25 PM.
You can also see there were other inventory transactions already affecting this item, including a Receive transaction on 3/2 at 12:52 PM, which impacted the running balance.
Because of that, the Perpetual Balance is not calculated by looking at only the two withdrawal rows by themselves. It is a running balance that reflects all transactions already recorded for that item.
That is why the balance does not simply calculate as 0 - 12 - 38 = -50 when reviewing the history.

In short:
  • The system did not ignore the balance
  • The transaction was allowed based on the inventory activity recorded at the time it was entered
  • The negative balance appearance is due to how the transaction was backdated into the historical timeline
Please let us know if you would like us to walk through the report with you line by line.
 
Thank you!

Hello Nganhai,


The Adjustment column shows the change that was made. For example, an adjustment of 12 means that 12 broken units were removed. If the inventory was at zero before that day, removing 12 would result in a perpetual balance of -12.
So, the Adjustment column reflects what was changed, and the Perpetual column shows the inventory balance after that change.
The only exception is when the entry is labeled as a Reconciliation. Since physical inventory is not a manual override, the quantity entered becomes the new perpetual balance, and the adjustment reflects the difference between what was previously recorded and what was physically counted.

Hello Nganhai,

Based on the report you provided, you’ll notice two columns: Actual Transaction Date and Transaction Date.
The transaction in question was entered on 3/20 at 2:24 PM, but it was backdated to 3/2. The system verifies inventory based on when the transaction is entered, not the backdated transaction date.
Since there was enough inventory available at the time of entry, the system allowed the quantity to be deducted. However, when you look at the backdated transaction date (3/2), it may show a negative balance because inventory levels were different on that date.
In summary, the Actual Transaction Date determines whether the transaction can be processed, while the Transaction Date affects how it appears in reports. Please let me know if you have any questions!

turns.body_html:
The district is experiencing a perpetual inventory discrepancy related to Item 1228 (hamburger buns). 

The item is currently showing 64 Whole Units (WU) in perpetual inventory when it should reflect 64 Broken Units (BU). Which does not really make sense to me, but anyways.

The discrepancy appears to stem from Transaction 23932, which is tied to a physical inventory count completed on 02/28/2026. The transaction was later modified on 03/17/2026.
  • count entered: 4 cases and 60 each
  • The quantity was subsequently altered to 64 units for unclear reasons
  • This change appears to have caused an unintended unit conversion or overwrite, resulting in incorrect perpetual inventory values
The transaction history shows the modification occurred after the physical inventory was finalized
No clear justification for the unit change has been identified

Requesting review to determine:
  • Why the physical inventory transaction was altered post-count
  • How/why the system converted or stored the quantity as WU instead of BU
